meta.title: Attention Is All You Need — journal club

slides.title: Attention Is All You Need
slides.authors: Vaswani, Shazeer, Parmar, Uszkoreit, Jones, Gomez, Kaiser, Polosukhin
slides.affiliation: Google Brain · Google Research · University of Toronto

slides.action_title: Recurrence forces sequential computation — the bottleneck this paper attacks
points: Dominant seq-transduction models (RNN/LSTM/GRU encoder–decoder) compute step by step along the sequence.
This sequential nature precludes parallelization within a training example and lengthens training.
Long-range dependencies must traverse many sequential steps, making them hard to learn.

slides.action_title: A model based entirely on attention — no recurrence, no convolution
points: The Transformer: the first sequence-transduction model relying solely on (self-)attention.
More parallelizable and faster to train than recurrent/convolutional models.
New state of the art: 28.4 BLEU (WMT'14 EN→DE) and 41.8 BLEU (EN→FR), trained in 3.5 days on 8 GPUs.

slides.action_title: The Transformer stacks self-attention + feed-forward, replacing recurrence
slides.annotation: Encoder & decoder are stacks of N = 6 identical layers; each layer = multi-head self-attention + position-wise FFN, with residual connections + layer normalization. Positional encodings inject order.

slides.action_title: Attention is a softmax-weighted sum of values, scaled by 1/√dₖ
slides.note: Scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps the dot products from growing large and pushing the softmax into low-gradient regions when $d_k$ is large.

slides.action_title: Multi-head attention runs h attention functions in parallel subspaces

figure.caption: Figure 2: (left) Scaled dot-product attention. (right) Multi-head attention.

slides.action_title: The big Transformer beats prior SOTA at a fraction of the training cost

table.caption: WMT 2014 newstest2014 BLEU and training cost (best per column in bold).
table.footnote: Values transcribed from Table 2 (p.8). Big model trained 3.5 days on 8× P100.

slides.action_title: Attention heads learn interpretable long-distance structure

figure.caption: Figure 3: An attention head following a long-distance dependency (verb 'making').

slides.annotation: Different heads specialize; several appear to track syntactic / anaphora structure (Figs. 3–5).

slides.action_title: What to push on — strengths granted, here are my concerns

points.head: O(n²) attention
points.body: Self-attention is quadratic in sequence length n in compute and memory; the paper itself flags restricted/local attention as future work for long inputs. (paper §7 + complexity table)

points.head: Positional encoding generalization
points.body: Sinusoidal PE is claimed to extrapolate to longer sequences than seen in training, but this is asserted more than tested. (my analysis)

points.head: Ablations tuned on dev
points.body: Table 3 variations are selected on newstest2013 (dev) — watch for dev-set overfitting of architectural choices. (my analysis)

points.head: Evaluation breadth
points.body: Evidence is two MT pairs + one parsing task; 'attention is all you need' is a broad claim relative to that scope. (my analysis)

slides.title: Questions for the room
questions: Does the $1/\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling still matter at modern $d_k$, or do LayerNorm + init absorb it?
How far does $O(n^2)$ attention scale before sparse/linear attention becomes mandatory?
Table 3(E) shows learned PE ≈ sinusoidal — so why keep sinusoidal as the default?

slides.title: References
entries: Vaswani et al. (2017). Attention Is All You Need. NeurIPS. arXiv:1706.03762.
